title: Dog Papilloma on Lip: Causes, Symptoms, and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about dog lip papillomas, why they appear, and when to seek veterinary care for these cauliflower-like growths.
content_html:
What are Canine Papillomas?

Canine papillomas, commonly referred to as dog warts, are benign growths caused by the canine papillomavirus. These growths typically appear as small, flesh-colored, cauliflower-like bumps on the lips, muzzle, or inside the mouth. They are most frequently seen in puppies and young dogs whose immune systems are still developing, or in older dogs with compromised immunity. While they may look concerning, they are viral in nature and generally painless.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a typical lip papilloma is Low. Most cases do not require an immediate trip to the emergency room. However, you should monitor the growth closely. If the papilloma begins to bleed excessively, shows signs of a secondary infection like pus or a foul odor, or if it grows so large that it prevents your dog from eating or drinking comfortably, you should schedule an appointment with your primary veterinarian.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of the growth is one of the most helpful things you can do for triage. A visual allows a veterinary professional to assess the texture, color, and location of the mass. Because many different types of skin growths can look similar, a photo helps differentiate a harmless viral wart from more serious conditions like mast cell tumors or histiocytomas, ensuring your dog receives the correct priority level for an exam.

faqs.question: Are dog warts contagious to humans?
faqs.answer: No. Canine papillomavirus is species-specific, meaning it can spread between dogs through direct contact or shared toys, but it cannot be transmitted to humans or other pets like cats.

faqs.question: How long do dog papillomas last?
faqs.answer: In most healthy dogs, the immune system will eventually recognize the virus and clear the growths naturally within 1 to 5 months.

faqs.question: Should I try to remove the wart at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to cut, pull, or use over-the-counter human wart removals on your dog. This can cause significant pain, bleeding, and severe infection.
